title: Advanced reactor definition and NRC context
text: NRC describes advanced reactors as including non-light-water reactor designs and small modular light-water reactors, with designs that may use passive safety features, alternative fuels or coolants, or smaller reactor sizes.
why_it_matters: Use this as context only. It does not prove any individual project is licensed or deployable.

title: NRC separates applicants, pre-applicants, guidance, and project information
text: The NRC advanced reactors page routes users to who the agency is working with, prospective applicant guidance, modernized guidance and policy, meetings, and project information.
why_it_matters: A credible memo should distinguish broad NRC advanced-reactor activity from a specific docketed application or project milestone.

title: Application project pages are stronger evidence than announcements
text: NRC applicant project pages identify advanced reactor applications under review and organize project overview, key milestones, public involvement, contacts, review documents, and dashboards where available.
why_it_matters: If a company claims a near-term deployment date, the analyst should look for a submitted and accepted application record, not only press releases.

title: Public milestones can anchor timeline diligence
text: NRC application project records typically distinguish tendering, acceptance review, docketing decisions, schedule letters, safety review, environmental review, hearings, and permit decisions.
why_it_matters: Deployment timelines should be benchmarked against public licensing milestones before being treated as bankable.

title: Pre-application engagement is a weaker signal than application review
text: NRC pre-application activities include white papers, technical reports, topical reports, readiness assessments, and other engagement before a formal application.
why_it_matters: A claim that NRC engagement is underway should be diligence-scored below a submitted or docketed construction permit or license application.

title: Inactive pre-application projects can lose signal value
text: NRC describes active pre-application engagement as involving a Regulatory Engagement Plan or substantial history of interactions, and notes that projects with no activity for a year may be deemed inactive.
why_it_matters: Investors should ask for current public engagement artifacts and not rely on stale pre-application references.

title: Readiness assessment identifies gaps but does not approve the project
text: NRC readiness assessments allow staff to identify information gaps, major technical or policy issues, and become familiar with a draft application, while supporting planning and resourcing.
why_it_matters: A readiness assessment can de-risk preparation but should not be represented as regulatory approval.

title: Readiness assessment does not predetermine docketing
text: NRC states that a readiness assessment does not predetermine whether an application will be accepted and docketed and does not provide regulatory determinations.
why_it_matters: This supports the memo conclusion that pre-application readiness is not equivalent to licensing maturity.

title: ARCAP/TI-CAP shows application content burden
text: NRC ARCAP guidance is technology-inclusive, risk-informed, and performance-based application guidance for advanced reactor applications, including construction permits, operating licenses, combined licenses, design certifications, standard design approvals, and manufacturing licenses.
why_it_matters: Credible deployment claims need evidence that the applicant can produce the required safety and licensing content.

title: Advanced reactor SAR content is nontrivial
text: TI-CAP developed content for portions of the safety analysis report informed by technology-inclusive risk-informed guidance for non-light-water reactor licensing basis development.
why_it_matters: This helps explain why generic claims of regulatory engagement do not prove schedule credibility.

title: Application pathway must be explicit
text: NRC advanced reactor application guidance supports prospective applicants using existing and emerging licensing paths such as Part 50, Part 52, and Part 53-related guidance.
why_it_matters: A diligence memo should ask which licensing path the counterparty is actually pursuing.

title: Guidance is not a substitute for application evidence
text: NRC guidance pages provide process and content expectations for applicants but do not establish that a particular project has met them.
why_it_matters: A project with only guidance alignment remains unsupported until application-specific evidence is available.

title: HALEU is regulated across production, use, storage, and transportation
date_or_status: Page updated for NRC new fuels program
text: NRC states that it will license commercial production, utilization, storage, and transportation of HALEU and HALEU-containing fuel.
why_it_matters: A HALEU fuel claim needs evidence beyond enrichment supply, including downstream handling, storage, and transport.

title: Advanced non-light-water reactors may require 5 to 20 percent HALEU
date_or_status: Page updated for NRC new fuels program
text: NRC notes that many advanced non-light-water reactor designs propose HALEU enriched between 5 and 20 weight percent U-235 for smaller cores, longer lives, and efficiency.
why_it_matters: Fuel-cycle diligence should ask for assay, form, quantity, delivery windows, and reactor-specific fuel qualification.

title: HALEU transport requires criticality evaluation
text: NRC explains that applicants must evaluate whether HALEU-containing fuel will maintain subcriticality during transportation.
why_it_matters: Transportation can be a gating dependency for first-core timing and should not be assumed solved.

title: UF6 above 5 percent requires additional review or exemption
text: NRC regulations require applicants for packages containing UF6 enriched above 5 weight percent U-235 to perform an additional criticality evaluation or request an exemption for NRC staff review.
why_it_matters: Fuel supply schedules need a package and transportation proof path.

title: HALEU requires safeguards and MC&A attention
source_name: NRC Security & Safeguards of New Fuels
text: NRC states that HALEU will require attention to physical security, safeguards, and material control and accounting, with requirements depending on enrichment and quantity.
why_it_matters: Security and safeguards evidence should be part of fuel-cycle readiness, especially for material near 10 to 20 percent U-235.

title: Category II requirements may apply
source_name: NRC Security & Safeguards of New Fuels
text: NRC describes that facilities with 10 kilograms or more of U-235 in uranium enriched to 10 to 20 weight percent U-235 are subject to Category II requirements.
why_it_matters: A project cannot be diligence-clean without understanding facility category and safeguards burden.

title: NRC is evaluating back-end framework for advanced fuels
source_name: NRC Storage and Disposal of New Fuels
text: NRC staff is seeking technical information and has released reports on near-term advanced reactor fuel concepts such as TRISO and metal fuels for transport, storage, and disposal questions.
why_it_matters: Back-end assumptions should be treated as diligence items rather than afterthoughts.

title: Storage and disposal are part of deployment credibility
source_name: NRC Storage and Disposal of New Fuels
text: NRC new-fuels storage and disposal work covers unirradiated fuel transport, storage of spent fuel, irradiated fuel transport, and disposal for advanced fuel concepts.
why_it_matters: A project memo should distinguish front-end fuel availability from total fuel-cycle readiness.

title: Fuel safety evidence supports reactor and fuel-cycle licensing
text: NRC new fuels safety work addresses safety considerations for advanced fuels and the information needed to support regulatory review.
why_it_matters: Novel fuel designs should be treated as a licensing and qualification dependency.

title: Fuel qualification can affect timeline
text: Advanced fuel safety and performance data can determine whether reactor and fuel facility reviews have enough evidence to proceed efficiently.
why_it_matters: If fuel qualification evidence is missing, the deployment timeline should be discounted.

title: DOE program supports HALEU supply chain, not project-specific proof
date_or_status: DOE program under Energy Act of 2020
text: DOE describes the HALEU Availability Program as supporting a diverse, market-based HALEU supply chain for civilian domestic research, demonstration, and commercial use.
why_it_matters: Program existence supports market context but does not prove one developer has bankable fuel supply.

title: HALEU availability is essential to advanced reactor demonstrations
date_or_status: DOE program under Energy Act of 2020
text: DOE identifies HALEU Availability Program activities as essential to demonstration and commercial deployment of advanced reactors, including demonstration projects supported by federal funding.
why_it_matters: Fuel availability should be a core kill-risk item in advanced reactor diligence.

title: DOE allocation recognizes supply gaps can delay deployment
date_or_status: DOE allocation process and conditional selections
text: DOE states that HALEU is not currently available from domestic suppliers at needed scale and that supply gaps could delay advanced reactor deployment.
why_it_matters: This directly supports treating HALEU as a schedule and bankability dependency.

title: Conditional allocations are not complete supply proof
date_or_status: DOE allocation process and conditional selections
text: DOE allocation process materials identify conditional selections and an ongoing process for interested parties to request HALEU from DOE.
why_it_matters: An allocation signal should be diligence-weighted below binding supply, fabrication, transport, and delivery evidence.

title: DOE consortium gathers demand and informs supply activities
date_or_status: DOE consortium for HALEU demand and supply coordination
text: DOE established the HALEU Consortium to inform activities to secure domestic HALEU supply through the Availability Program, including demand estimates for domestic commercial use.
why_it_matters: Demand estimates and consortium membership are useful signals but not financeable offtake or supply agreements.

title: Limited domestic capacity increases investment risk
date_or_status: DOE consortium for HALEU demand and supply coordination
text: DOE says very limited domestic capacity to provide HALEU is an obstacle to advanced reactor development and deployment and increases investment risk.
why_it_matters: This supports a diligence finding that fuel-cycle path is a major unresolved dependency.

title: NuScale shows a completed NRC design certification safety review
date_or_status: NRC completed safety review; page reviewed February 11, 2025
text: NRC states that staff completed the safety review of NuScale US600 small modular reactor design certification application.
why_it_matters: This is a benchmark for the public evidence expected behind a mature design-review claim.

title: Design certification does not prove commercial project success
date_or_status: NRC completed safety review; page reviewed February 11, 2025
text: NuScale US600 documentation concerns a standard design certification for a 12-module SMR design, separate from site, offtake, financing, and construction execution.
why_it_matters: Comparable precedent should separate regulatory design maturity from project bankability.

title: RAIs are part of mature licensing evidence
source_name: NuScale US600 application documents and RAIs
text: NRC NuScale application documents include the final safety analysis report, NRC documents, requests for additional information, safety evaluations, and approval materials.
why_it_matters: A serious licensing record generates detailed public information exchange, not just high-level statements.

title: Application documents reveal technical review depth
source_name: NuScale US600 application documents and RAIs
text: NuScale application document pages organize the design certification application and applicant/NRC review documents under the NRC Part 52 process.
why_it_matters: This supports asking counterparties for public docket references and issue trackers.

title: Hermes construction permit is an advanced reactor test precedent
text: NRC issued a construction permit for the Hermes test reactor to Kairos Power on December 14, 2023, for a low-power non-electric test reactor in Oak Ridge, Tennessee.
why_it_matters: Hermes is a useful precedent, but the test-reactor path should not be overgeneralized to commercial power campuses.

title: Hermes uses TRISO fuel and HALEU
text: NRC describes Hermes as a fluoride salt-cooled high-temperature test reactor using TRISO fuel particles in pebble form and HALEU.
why_it_matters: This helps benchmark fuel-type, test-reactor, and licensing-path claims.

title: Kemmerer shows a public construction permit application record
source_name: TerraPower / Natrium Kemmerer construction permit application
date_or_status: Application tendered March 28, 2024; NRC review milestones posted
text: NRC states it is engaged in review activities for TerraPower/US SFR Owner construction permit application for Kemmerer Power Station Unit 1, tendered March 28, 2024.
why_it_matters: A near-term commercial deployment claim should have public artifacts comparable to an application record and review schedule.

title: Natrium precedent includes DOE-backed demonstration and fuel dependencies
source_name: TerraPower / Natrium Kemmerer construction permit application
date_or_status: Application tendered March 28, 2024; NRC review milestones posted
text: NRC describes Kemmerer as part of DOE Advanced Reactor Demonstration Program and associated with TerraPower and GE Hitachi Natrium sodium fast reactor technology.
why_it_matters: Public demonstration backing still leaves licensing, fuel, construction, and financing diligence questions.

title: TRISO-X is a fuel fabrication licensing benchmark
text: NRC lists TRISO-X as a uranium fuel fabrication facility in Oak Ridge, Tennessee with facility status as Licensing Application and docket number 07007027.
why_it_matters: Fuel fabrication capacity should be diligence-tested as its own licensed facility path.

title: TRISO-X RAIs show fuel facility review depth
text: NRC TRISO-X materials list requests for additional information on human factors, quality assurance, structural, emergency plan, safeguards, and other licensing topics.
why_it_matters: Supplier or developer claims about fabrication readiness need docketed facility evidence, not just partnership announcements.

title: Fuel fabrication is independently regulated
text: NRC explains that fuel fabrication facilities convert enriched uranium into fuel for reactors and are regulated across several fuel fabrication categories.
why_it_matters: Advanced reactor projects need evidence of fabrication licensing and interface readiness.

title: Several new HALEU fuel fabrication applications are Category II
text: NRC notes that several new proposed HALEU fuel fabrication applications will be Category II facilities, including applications such as TRISO-X, Kairos Power Atlas, and Ultra Safe Nuclear.
why_it_matters: This supports treating fabrication, safeguards, and facility licensing as core fuel-cycle diligence items.
